items.title: Spike GitNexus: Graph RAG over Commonweave or AgentStack repo
items.source_title: abhigyanpatwari/GitNexus — Client-side Graph RAG over any GitHub repo, zero server
items.body: GitNexus builds an interactive knowledge graph over a GitHub repo entirely client-side — no server, no API keys. Point it at the Commonweave repo (C:/Users/simon/.openclaw/workspace/commonweave/) or the AgentStack module (modules/agentstack/) and see if the graph surface is genuinely useful for onboarding or cross-module reasoning. The test question: does it tell you something faster than grepping or reading CONTEXT.md manually? If yes, it becomes a research tool for any new client codebase Simon needs to understand quickly.
prerequisites: public or locally-accessible GitHub repo URL
items.first_step: Open https://github.com/abhigyanpatwari/GitNexus in a browser, read the README top-to-bottom (2 min), then try the live demo link if one exists — no install yet.

items.title: Evaluate Warp agentic terminal: does it replace any Larry CLI tooling?
items.source_title: warpdotdev/warp — Agentic development environment, born out of the terminal
items.body: Warp is a Rust terminal going fully agentic — 11K+ stars in a day signals real momentum. The question for Simon's stack is narrow: does Warp's agentic layer do anything that Claude Code CLI + PowerShell can't already do, specifically around cron monitoring, multi-pane session management, or shell history search? Skim the README and their 'Warp AI' feature page. If it's just a prettier terminal with autocomplete, it's a track. If it has agent-to-shell hooks that could replace the gateway.cmd retry loop or simplify cron debugging, it's worth a 30-min install spike.
prerequisites: Windows build available (check Warp site — was Mac-only historically)
items.first_step: Run: curl -s https://api.github.com/repos/warpdotdev/warp/releases/latest | node -e "const d=require('fs').readFileSync('/dev/stdin','utf8');const r=JSON.parse(d);console.log(r.tag_name, r.assets.map(a=>a.name).join(', '))" to check if a Windows binary shipped in the latest release.
